name: Pozuelo de Calatrava, Miguelturra y Ermita de Santa Marina — circular desde Almagro
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Almagro traverse the diverse landscapes of Castilla-La Mancha, characterized by immense plains, interspersed mountainous areas, and river valleys. The region offers varied terrain, from gentle slopes ideal for relaxed rides to more demanding segments with significant elevation changes. Cyclists can explore routes that pass through the iconic La Mancha plains and near unique volcanic lagoons. Best road cycling routes around Almagro The most popular road cycling route is Pozuelo de Calatrava, Miguelturra y Ermita de Santa Marina — circular desde Almagro, a 53.4 miles (85.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 25 minutes to complete. This route offers a moderate challenge across varied terrain, passing through local towns. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Bolaños, Torralba y Carrión de Calatrava — circular desde Almagro, an easy 27.9 miles (45.0 km) path. This route features mostly flat sections, ideal for a less strenuous ride through the plains. Local road cyclists also love the Church of San Juan Bautista – Villar del Pozo loop from Almagro, a 60.1 miles (96.7 km) trail leading through the broader Almagro countryside, often completed in about 3 hours 58 minutes. Road cycling around Almagro is defined by the vast La Mancha Plains, proximity to volcanic lagoons, and routes that follow parts of the historic Ruta de Don Quijote. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging excursions. The routes in Almagro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almagro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Almagro?
A: Road cycling routes around Almagro primarily traverse the vast La Mancha plains, offering picturesque and often flat sections ideal for long-distance rides. However, the region also features interspersed mountainous areas and river valleys, providing varied terrain with gentle slopes for relaxed rides and more demanding segments with significant elevation changes for experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy ride?
A: Yes, Almagro offers routes suitable for various fitness levels. For an easier ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249647502/bolanos-torralba-y-carrion-de-calatrava-circular-desde-almagro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bolaños, Torralba y Carrión de Calatrava — circular desde Almagro&lt;/a&gt; route, which is 27.9 miles (45.0 km) long and features mostly flat sections through the plains.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5298825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of San Juan Bautista – Piedrabuena loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt;, spanning 115.5 km with significant elevation, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40015429" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Molino de Melocho – Villarrubia de los Ojos loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt;, which is 117.4 km long, offer more demanding excursions through the region's varied terrain.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while road cycling in Almagro?
A: Many routes pass through the iconic La Mancha plains. You can also find routes near unique salty lagoons of volcanic origin, such as Argamasilla, Inesperada, and Laguna del Prado. Some routes may also offer views of the Montes de Toledo in the broader region. For specific points of interest, you might encounter highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3420172" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Churri Summit&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2693294" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro de la Illozosa&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes starting from Almagro?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Almagro are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1251905687/pozuelo-de-calatrava-miguelturra-y-ermita-de-santa-marina-circular-desde-almagro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozuelo de Calatrava, Miguelturra y Ermita de Santa Marina — circular desde Almagro&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5298822" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of San Juan Bautista – Villar del Pozo loop from Almagro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Almagro?
A: The Castilla-La Mancha region generally offers pleasant conditions for cycling during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, especially in the plains, while winters are typically mild but can have cooler days. Checking the local weather forecast before your ride is always recommended.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Almagro?
A: The road cycling routes in Almagro are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from the vast plains to the unique volcanic lagoons, and the variety of routes catering to different ability levels.

Q: Are there any specific cultural or historical sites along the road cycling routes?
A: While the routes focus on natural landscapes, Almagro is situated in the historic Campo de Calatrava region, known for its castles and towns. Some routes follow parts of the historic Ruta de Don Quijote, allowing you to immerse yourself in the landscapes that inspired Miguel de Cervantes and potentially pass through charming villages with whitewashed windmills.

Q: Is Almagro well-connected by public transport for cyclists?
A: Almagro has public transport connections, but specific information regarding the transport of bicycles on trains or buses should be verified with the respective operators in advance. It's advisable to check local regulations and availability if you plan to combine cycling with public transport.

Q: Are there any specific regulations or permits needed for road cycling in protected areas around Almagro?
A: While general road cycling typically doesn't require permits, if your route takes you through or near protected natural areas like Tablas de Daimiel National Park or Lagunas de Ruidera Natural Park, it's always wise to check for any specific regulations regarding access or cycling within those zones. These areas are primarily for nature observation and may have restrictions on certain activities.

Q: Where can I find parking for my car when starting a road cycling route in Almagro?
A: Almagro, as a historic town, offers various parking options. You can typically find public parking areas within or on the outskirts of the town. It's recommended to check local signage for parking regulations and availability, especially in the historic center, to ensure a convenient starting point for your ride.
tips: Destroyed in the 1936 war, it also housed the Brotherhood of Santiago, since its hermitage disappeared in the 19th century. On July 23, 1983, the new hermitage was inaugurated on the ruins of the old one, the porch treated with white concrete set in flexible formwork stands out.

A town located in the center of the province of Ciudad Real, within the natural region of Campo de Calatrava, it is one of the municipalities with the highest rate of population growth. He has the village of Peralbillo separated from his territory, sixteen kilometers to the north. Highway CM-4174

A religious building dating from the 17th–20th centuries and mixes a popular style with a Latin cross plan. Its interior does not stand out for its extravagance but for its simplicity, with its mud walls and reinforced masonry in the corners. It has limestone ashlars and its central vault stands out, which is the most recent construction inside the Church.

We found an urban nucleus made up of very small houses with barely one floor. The white of the facades also stands out, which still survives together with the roofs and the traditional bars. Regarding its religious heritage, the Church of the Virgen de la Consolación stands out, which is open during worship hours. Other religious buildings are the hermitages of San Isidro Labrador and the Beato San Fernando de Ayala.

The careful exterior appearance of this small hermitage is striking, rebuilt at the beginning of the 20th century and recently restored. Despite this, access to the small temple can only be done on foot. The path is practically lost and neglected between farmland and vegetation, so much so that reaching it on foot is practically cross-country. Source: Mancha Ignota

The parish church of San Juan Bautista is a temple from the 18th century, with a Latin cross and a single nave. As annexes it has a tower, attached to the foot of the church and sacristy, attached to the right side of the temple and to one of the arms of the transept. The roof is made of curved tiles, and the structure is made of masonry on the walls, ashlar on the buttresses and brick on the arches.

Of the interior it is necessary to emphasize the half orange dome on pendentives that finish off the transept. The structure is supported by four stone columns, two of them uncovered and two plastered.
